questions.question: Zero-shot classification with CLIP requires no task-specific training. How does it work at inference?
options: Run a classifier head trained on ImageNet and remap class indices
Fine-tune the image encoder on a single labelled example per class

Encode a natural-language prompt like 'a photo of a {class}' for each candidate class, encode the test image, take argmax of cosine similarities between the image embedding and all class text embeddings
Search a database of labelled prototype images using nearest-neighbour lookup
questions.explanation: Zero-shot means no task-specific training: encode one prompt per class, encode the query image, argmax over cosine similarities. The only tuning lever is prompt engineering — using multiple templates per class and averaging their embeddings typically gains 1–3 top-1 points on ImageNet, as the OpenAI CLIP paper showed with 80 templates.

questions.question: CLIP's training objective builds an N×N similarity matrix from a batch of N (image, caption) pairs. What is the target for the diagonal vs off-diagonal entries?
options: The diagonal (matching pairs) should have high similarity and off-diagonal (non-matching) should have low similarity; the symmetric cross-entropy loss trains both encoders to achieve this simultaneously for image-to-text and text-to-image retrieval
All entries should converge to the same value for a well-calibrated model
The diagonal should be zero for numerical stability during early training
Only the first row and column are optimisation targets; the rest are regularisation
questions.explanation: The NxN matrix has N correct (i, i) pairs on the diagonal and N²-N incorrect (i, j≠i) pairs off-diagonal. The symmetric loss treats the diagonal row as the positive class for both loss_i2t (classify columns) and loss_t2i (classify rows). This directly trains both retrieval directions to be correct.

questions.question: In the TwoTower model, logit_scale is initialised as nn.Parameter(torch.ones([]) * 2.6592). Why that specific value?
options: It is the default PyTorch parameter initialisation for scalar parameters
It ensures gradient stability by keeping the scale parameter greater than 1.0
It matches the CLIP paper's reported learning rate schedule
ln(1/0.07) ≈ 2.6592, so logit_scale.exp() starts at 1/0.07 ≈ 14.3 — the CLIP default temperature tau=0.07 expressed as a learned inverse-temperature that the optimiser can adjust during training
questions.explanation: CLIP makes tau a learned parameter (logit_scale = 1/tau). Initialising at ln(1/0.07) means the model starts at the manually-tuned temperature from the original paper. Because it is a nn.Parameter, the optimiser can raise or lower it. The paper clips logit_scale.exp() at 100 to prevent collapse to zero temperature.

questions.question: In the zero_shot_classify function, after computing sim = i @ t.T, what produces the final class prediction?
options: Apply softmax to sim and sample from the resulting distribution
Take sim.argmax(dim=-1) to get the index of the most similar class text embedding, then look up the name in class_names
Run beam search over the text encoder conditioned on the top-5 similarity scores
Compute L2 distance between image and text embeddings and return the argmin
questions.explanation: zero_shot_classify computes the cosine similarity between the image embedding and all class text embeddings in one matrix multiply. argmax on dim=-1 picks the class with the highest similarity for each query image, then indexes into class_names. No softmax is needed because we only want the argmax, not calibrated probabilities.

questions.question: SigLIP replaces CLIP's softmax with a per-pair sigmoid loss. What practical benefit does this give?
options: Faster GPU kernels that reduce wall-clock training time by 3×
Better accuracy specifically on the COCO object detection benchmark
Lower peak GPU memory because the NxN matrix is never materialised
The sigmoid loss is per-pair and needs no batch-level normalisation denominator, so SigLIP trains well at small batch sizes where CLIP's softmax starves for negatives
questions.explanation: CLIP's symmetric cross-entropy is a softmax over the full batch: effective negatives = batch_size − 1. At batch 128, that is only 127 negatives. SigLIP treats each (image, caption) pair as a binary classification — match or not — with no dependence on other pairs in the batch. This decoupling lets SigLIP match CLIP quality at 10–100× smaller batch sizes.

questions.question: A practitioner reports 88% zero-shot top-1 on CIFAR-10 with one template and 90% with 80 templates per class. Why does template averaging help?
options: Different templates activate different aspects of the text encoder's learned distribution; averaging smooths the class embedding over the manifold of plausible natural-language descriptions, producing a more robust centroid that is less sensitive to individual-prompt quirks
Averaging 80 templates effectively doubles the training dataset seen by the model
It reduces variance in logit_scale because the text encoder sees more inputs
80 is the magic number discovered by the CLIP paper; fewer or more templates degrade performance
questions.explanation: 'A photo of a dog' emphasises one visual context; 'a blurry photo of a dog' another; 'a sketch of a dog' yet another. Averaging text embeddings over templates gives a smoother, more central representation of the concept that handles diverse test-image styles. The CLIP paper published 80 per-class templates that lift ImageNet zero-shot accuracy by ~2 points over a single template.
